nodes.title: Define the determinant of a square matrix
nodes.brief: Recognise the determinant as a unique number associated with each square matrix, written |A| or det A.
nodes.quote: f(A) is called the determinant of A. It is also denoted by |A| or det A
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The determinant is a single number, not a matrix.
For any square matrix A, the determinant (written |A|, det A, or Δ) is a scalar (real or complex) attached to A. Only square matrices have determinants; rectangular matrices do not.

PART 2 — Do not read |A| as modulus of A.
The notation |A| means determinant of A, which can be negative (e.g. |[[1,2],[3,4]]| = 1×4 − 2×3 = −2). Modulus is always non-negative; the determinant need not be.
keyMoves: Recognise the determinant as a single number attached to a square matrix
Write it as |A|, det A or Delta
Remember only square matrices have determinants
misconceptions: Reading |A| as the modulus of A
Trying to take the determinant of a non-square matrix

refreshers.answer: A scalar is one single number. 'Real or complex' just says the value is an ordinary number on the number line (and, in advanced cases, possibly a complex number). For Class 12 determinants with real entries you always get one real number.
ladder: Think about ordinary numbers you already use: 3, -2, 0, 1.5, the square root of 2. These are real numbers - any point on the number line.
A 'scalar' is just a fancy word for a single such number, used to contrast with a whole array of numbers like a matrix.

'Real or complex' means the determinant is allowed to be a real number, or in rarer cases a complex number (involving the square root of -1). For this chapter, with real entries, the determinant will always come out a real number.
The key point: a determinant collapses a whole square grid of numbers down to one single value.
refreshers.trigger: Student is unsure what a 'number (real or complex)' or 'scalar' means when told the determinant is one.
refreshers.returnCue: So |A| is one number, not a matrix - now let's see how to compute that number.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: When we say the determinant is just 'a number', what kinds of numbers are we allowing?

refreshers.trigger: Student is told the determinant |A| can be negative but does not recall what 'modulus' / absolute value |x| means or that it is always non-negative.
refreshers.surfacingQuestion: quick one before we go on: when you see the bars |...| around an ordinary number like |-3|, what do they do to it?
ladder: Think about a plain number with the bars around it: |5| = 5, and |-3| = 3. What happened to the minus sign in the second one?
Right - the bars strip the sign and just give you the size of the number. So |x| can never come out negative; the smallest it ever gets is 0.
That is the 'modulus' (or absolute value) of x: distance from 0 on the number line, always 0 or positive.
Now here is the twist: in |A|, those same bars do NOT mean modulus. |A| can be negative (we saw |[[1,2],[3,4]]| = -2). Same symbol, completely different job.
refreshers.answer: For an ordinary number, |x| is its modulus (absolute value): you throw away the sign and keep the size, so |-3| = 3 and the result is always non-negative. But the bars in |A| mean 'determinant of A', and a determinant is free to be negative - that is exactly why the chapter warns you not to read |A| as a modulus. So when you see a determinant come out as -2, do not 'fix' it to +2; the minus sign is real and meaningful.
refreshers.returnCue: so keep those two readings of the bars apart: |A| is the determinant (sign allowed), not a modulus - now back to what that single number really is.

nodes.title: Evaluate a 2 x 2 determinant
nodes.brief: Compute a second-order determinant as a11 a22 - a21 a12.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The formula: main-diagonal product minus off-diagonal product.
For A = [[a11, a12],[a21, a22]], the determinant is |A| = a11*a22 − a21*a12. Multiply the top-left and bottom-right entries (main diagonal), then subtract the product of the top-right and bottom-left entries (off-diagonal). The result is a single number.

PART 2 — Watch sign when the off-diagonal product is negative.
For [[2, 4],[−1, 2]], |A| = 2(2) − 4(−1) = 4 + 4 = 8. Subtracting a negative entry gives addition. Do not reverse the subtraction order: a11*a22 − a12*a21 is correct, not a12*a21 − a11*a22.
keyMoves: Multiply the main-diagonal entries a11 and a22
Subtract the product of the off-diagonal entries a21 and a12
Report the single number a11 a22 - a21 a12
misconceptions: Adding the two products instead of subtracting
Subtracting in the wrong order (off-diagonal minus diagonal)

nodes.title: Find the minor of an element
nodes.brief: Form the minor M_ij of a_ij by deleting its row and column and taking the resulting determinant.
nodes.quote: Minor of an element  $a_{ij}$  of a determinant is the determinant obtained by deleting its *i*th row and *j*th column in which element  $a_{ij}$  lies.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Delete the whole row and whole column of the element, then evaluate the remaining determinant.
The minor M_ij of element a_ij is the determinant of the sub-matrix formed by deleting row i and column j entirely. For a 3×3 matrix, each minor is a 2×2 determinant. Example: M_12 in a 3×3 is found by crossing out all of row 1 and all of column 2, leaving a 2×2 sub-matrix.

PART 2 — Delete the entire row and column, not just the single entry.
A common mistake is deleting only the element a_ij itself. You must remove every entry in row i and every entry in column j. The minor is the determinant of what remains.
keyMoves: Locate the element a_ij in its row i and column j
Delete that whole row and that whole column
Evaluate the smaller determinant that remains as M_ij
misconceptions: Deleting only the single element rather than the whole row and column
Confusing the minor (a determinant) with the element itself

nodes.title: Find the cofactor of an element
nodes.brief: Form the cofactor A_ij = (-1)^(i+j) M_ij by attaching the chequerboard sign to the minor.
nodes.quote: Cofactor of an element  $a_{ij}$ , denoted by  $A_{ij}$  is defined by  $A_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}$ , where  $M_{ij}$  is minor of  $a_{ij}$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Cofactor = sign factor times minor: A_ij = (-1)^(i+j) * M_ij.
The cofactor of element a_ij is the minor M_ij multiplied by a sign determined by position. The sign is (+1) when i+j is even and (-1) when i+j is odd. This gives the chequerboard pattern: + − + / − + − / + − + for a 3×3 matrix.

PART 2 — Apply the chequerboard sign, do not confuse cofactor with minor.
M_ij and A_ij agree when i+j is even; they differ only in sign when i+j is odd. Example: for position (1,2), i+j=3 (odd), so A_12 = −M_12. For position (2,2), i+j=4 (even), so A_22 = +M_22.
keyMoves: Compute the minor M_ij
Multiply by (-1)^(i+j): + when i + j is even, - when odd
Report A_ij = (-1)^(i+j) M_ij
misconceptions: Forgetting the sign factor and equating the cofactor to the minor
Getting the sign wrong for positions where i + j is odd

nodes.title: Expand a 3 x 3 determinant along a row or column
nodes.brief: Evaluate a third-order determinant by cofactor expansion along any row or column.
nodes.quote: Hence, expanding a determinant along any row or column gives same value.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Expand along any row or column: multiply each element by its cofactor and sum.
To evaluate |A| for a 3×3 matrix, choose any row (or column) and compute the sum of each element multiplied by its cofactor. Expanding along row 1: |A| = a_11*A_11 + a_12*A_12 + a_13*A_13. Each A_ij is the signed 2×2 minor computed earlier.

PART 2 — Choose the row or column with the most zeros to minimise arithmetic.
All six expansions (3 rows, 3 columns) give the same value — this is a theorem. Expand along whichever row/column has the most zeros, since zero times a cofactor contributes nothing. Do not mix elements from one row with cofactors from another row.
keyMoves: Choose a row or column (ideally one with zeros)
Multiply each entry by its cofactor and sum
Trust that any row/column expansion gives the same answer
misconceptions: Forgetting the alternating signs while expanding
Thinking different rows/columns give different determinant values

nodes.title: Find the area of a triangle using a determinant
nodes.brief: Use the determinant of vertex coordinates to get triangle area; collinear points give a zero determinant.
nodes.quote: The area of the triangle formed by three collinear points is zero.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Area = half the absolute value of the vertex determinant.
For a triangle with vertices (x1,y1), (x2,y2), (x3,y3), the area is (1/2)*|D| where D is the determinant with rows (x1,y1,1), (x2,y2,1), (x3,y3,1). The factor 1/2 and the absolute value are both essential — area is never negative.

PART 2 — A zero determinant means the points are collinear.
If D = 0, the 'triangle' has zero area, so the three points lie on one straight line. Setting D = 0 is therefore the collinearity test, and it also yields the equation of the line through two given points (treat the third vertex as (x,y)).
keyMoves: Form the determinant with rows (x_i, y_i, 1) for the three vertices
Take one half of its absolute value for the area
Set the determinant to zero to test collinearity / find a line
misconceptions: Forgetting the factor 1/2 or the absolute value
Thinking collinear points give a non-zero area

nodes.title: Find the adjoint of a square matrix
nodes.brief: Form adj A as the transpose of the matrix of cofactors.
nodes.quote: The adjoint of a square matrix  $A = [a_{ij}]_{n \times n}$  is defined as the transpose of the matrix  $[A_{ij}]_{n \times n}$ , where  $A_{ij}$  is the cofactor of the element  $a_{ij}$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Two steps: cofactor matrix, then transpose.
The adjoint of a square matrix A, written adj A, is the transpose of the matrix of cofactors [A_ij]. Step 1: compute each signed cofactor A_ij = (-1)^(i+j) times the minor M_ij. Step 2: arrange these into the cofactor matrix and then transpose it.

PART 2 — 2x2 shortcut: swap diagonal, negate off-diagonal.
For A = [[a,b],[c,d]], adj A = [[d,-b],[-c,a]]. The two diagonal entries swap and the two off-diagonal entries change sign. This shortcut only applies to 2x2.

PART 3 — Do not forget the transpose step.
The cofactor matrix and adj A differ (unless A is symmetric): adj A has (i,j) entry equal to A_ji (the cofactor of the TRANSPOSED position). Stopping at the cofactor matrix without transposing is the most common error.
keyMoves: Compute the cofactor A_ij of every element
Arrange them into the cofactor matrix [A_ij]
Transpose it to get adj A
misconceptions: Forgetting to transpose the cofactor matrix
Using the minors instead of the signed cofactors

nodes.title: Use the identity A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A| I
nodes.brief: Apply the key theorem linking a matrix, its adjoint, and its determinant.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The identity: A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A| I.
For any square matrix A of order n, multiplying A by its adjoint (in either order) gives the scalar |A| times the identity matrix. The result is |A| I — NOT just I. This holds because a row of A dotted with its own cofactors sums to |A|, while a row dotted with another row's cofactors sums to 0.

PART 2 — Divide by |A| to reach the inverse — only when |A| is non-zero.
From A(adj A) = |A| I, dividing by |A| gives A * ((1/|A|) adj A) = I, so the inverse is (1/|A|) adj A. This division is valid only when |A| != 0. A common error is writing A(adj A) = I, dropping the |A| factor.
keyMoves: Recall A(adj A) = (adj A)A = |A| I
Recognise the off-diagonal sums of element times cofactor vanish
Divide by |A| (when non-zero) to head toward the inverse
misconceptions: Writing A(adj A) = I instead of |A| I
Dividing by |A| even when |A| = 0

nodes.title: Recognise a singular matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a singular matrix as a square matrix with determinant zero.
nodes.quote: A square matrix A is said to be singular if  $|\mathbf{A}| = 0$ .
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Singular means determinant zero.
A square matrix A is singular if |A| = 0. To test, compute the determinant and check whether it equals zero. Example: [[1,2],[4,8]] has |A| = 1*8 - 2*4 = 8 - 8 = 0, so it is singular.

PART 2 — A singular matrix has no inverse.
Singularity is exactly the obstruction to invertibility: since the inverse formula divides by |A|, a zero determinant makes the inverse undefined. Singular = non-invertible. The rows (or columns) of a singular matrix are linearly dependent (e.g. row 2 = 4 times row 1 above).
keyMoves: Compute the determinant |A|
If |A| = 0, declare A singular
Conclude a singular matrix has no inverse
misconceptions: Calling a matrix singular when its determinant is non-zero
Thinking a singular matrix can still be inverted

nodes.title: Recognise a non-singular matrix
nodes.brief: Identify a non-singular matrix as a square matrix with non-zero determinant.
nodes.quote: A square matrix A is said to be non-singular if  $|A| \neq 0$
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Non-singular means determinant non-zero.
A square matrix A is non-singular if |A| != 0. To test, compute the determinant and confirm it is not zero. Example: [[2,1],[3,4]] has |A| = 8 - 3 = 5 != 0, so it is non-singular.

PART 2 — Non-singular = invertible.
Non-singularity is exactly the condition for invertibility: a non-singular matrix has the well-defined inverse (1/|A|) adj A. Because |A| != 0, you can divide the adjoint identity A(adj A) = |A| I by |A| to obtain the inverse. Singular (|A|=0) and non-singular (|A|!=0) are the two mutually exclusive cases.
keyMoves: Compute the determinant |A|
If |A| is not 0, declare A non-singular
Conclude a non-singular matrix is invertible
misconceptions: Confusing the non-singular condition (|A| not 0) with singular (|A| = 0)
Thinking any square matrix is automatically non-singular

nodes.title: Use the product rule |AB| = |A| |B|
nodes.brief: Apply the theorem that the determinant of a product is the product of the determinants.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The determinant of a product is the product of determinants.
For square matrices A and B of the same order, |AB| = |A| |B|. This is a multiplicative rule — NOT additive. Example: if |A| = 3 and |B| = 4, then |AB| = 12, regardless of the individual entries.

PART 2 — Use it to reason about invertibility.
From AB = I, take determinants: |A||B| = |I| = 1, which forces |A| != 0 (and |B| != 0). So if a product of matrices is the identity, each factor must be non-singular. The product of two non-singular matrices is itself non-singular. Do not write |AB| = |A| + |B|.
keyMoves: Apply |AB| = |A| |B| for square matrices of the same order
Use |I| = 1 to deduce |A| |B| = 1 from AB = I
Conclude products of non-singular matrices stay non-singular
misconceptions: Writing |AB| = |A| + |B| instead of the product
Applying the rule to non-square or mismatched matrices

nodes.title: Classify a system as consistent or inconsistent
nodes.brief: Call a system consistent if a solution exists, inconsistent if none does.
nodes.quote: A system of equations is said to be *consistent* if its solution (one or more) exists.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Consistent means a solution exists; inconsistent means none does.
Write the system as AX = B. If a solution (one or infinitely many) exists, the system is consistent; if no solution exists, it is inconsistent. The determinant |A| decides which case you are in.

PART 2 — Use |A| to classify, then test (adj A)B if |A| = 0.
If |A| != 0, A is invertible and the system has the unique solution X = A-inverse B (consistent). If |A| = 0, compute (adj A)B: if it is NOT the zero matrix the system is inconsistent (no solution); if it IS the zero matrix the system has infinitely many solutions (consistent, dependent).
keyMoves: Write the system as AX = B
If |A| is not 0, solve uniquely by X = A-inverse B (consistent)
If |A| = 0, test (adj A)B to decide consistency
misconceptions: Calling an inconsistent system consistent because equations 'look solvable'
Using X = A-inverse B when |A| = 0 (the inverse does not exist)

nodes.title: Invert a matrix via A-inverse = (1/|A|) adj A using non-singularity
nodes.brief: Use the criterion 'A is invertible iff A is non-singular' and the formula A-inverse = (1/|A|) adj A.
nodes.quote: A square matrix A is invertible if and only if A is nonsingular matrix.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — A is invertible if and only if |A| != 0 (non-singular).
The capstone result: a square matrix has an inverse exactly when its determinant is non-zero. Proof sketch: |AB| = |A||B|, so AB = I forces |A||B| = 1, meaning |A| != 0; conversely the adjoint identity supplies an explicit inverse when |A| != 0.

PART 2 — Compute the inverse: A-inverse = (1/|A|) adj A.
Three steps: (1) check |A| != 0 (else no inverse exists); (2) compute adj A as the transpose of the cofactor matrix; (3) divide by |A|. The same machinery solves AX = B via X = A-inverse B. Do not attempt to invert a singular matrix (|A| = 0), and do not forget to divide the adjoint by |A|.
keyMoves: Check |A| is non-zero (non-singular) so the inverse exists
Compute adj A (transpose of the cofactor matrix)
Form A-inverse = (1/|A|) adj A, and solve AX = B by X = A-inverse B
misconceptions: Attempting to invert a singular matrix (|A| = 0)
Forgetting to divide the adjoint by the determinant
